title: Last week, the California Legislature passed Assemblymember Irwin’s Community College Placement Bill, AB 705, which changes the way in which students are placed in remedial courses
prompt_preview:

raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "Thousands of students every semester are wrongly placed into remedial courses that unnecessarily delay their timelines toward graduation.",
    "Placement tests as the sole indicator for student placement put California’s students at a disadvantage.",
    "Allowing students to be placed based on the best of multiple measures, including high school coursework, enables them to rise to the challenge and transfer or start their careers more quickly.",
    "80% of students entering Community College enroll in at least one remedial level course in English, math, or both.",
    "A majority of students enrolled in remedial courses are minority students who have higher dropout rates due to long sequences of remedial courses.",
    "Developmental courses are considered to be one of the largest impediments to student success in California’s community colleges due to the added time required to complete these courses.",
    "The increased cost to students associated with developmental courses negatively impacts their educational outcomes.",
    "The psychological effect of not feeling challenged by coursework is significant among students in remedial courses.",
    "The use of standardized assessment tests as the primary factor in placing students in math and English is ineffective."
  ]
}
```
